content.text: Beta Graduation Criteria and Decision Forum

Beta graduation is the decision to promote a Claude integration from a controlled, limited rollout to general availability. It is a gate, not a timeline: a feature graduates when it meets the graduation criteria, not when it has been in beta for a predetermined number of weeks. The decision forum is the structured meeting at which the graduation decision is made and documented. Both the criteria and the forum exist to prevent two common failure modes: graduating features too early (pushing unstable or low-quality integrations to the full user base) and delaying graduation indefinitely (leaving features in beta limbo while the team defers the decision to avoid accountability for a final quality assessment).

attrs.alt: Beta graduation gate: beta leads to graduation gate which either passes to GA or fails to extended beta, with option to retire from extended beta

content.text: The gate has three outcomes: pass to general availability, extend beta to address identified blockers, or retire the feature. Retirement from beta is rare but valid — a feature that has been in extended beta for multiple cycles with no viable path to meeting graduation criteria should be retired rather than allowed to consume ongoing maintenance resources. The graduation decision forum documents whichever outcome is chosen and the criteria that drove the decision.

Graduation criteria must be defined before the beta begins, not at the time of the graduation decision. Criteria defined after data collection is complete are subject to selection bias: the team unconsciously sets thresholds that the actual data happens to meet or misses by enough to justify further work. Pre-committing to criteria before launch eliminates this bias and creates a clear contract between the product team and stakeholders about what success looks like.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum

class CriterionStatus(str, Enum):
    MET       = "met"
    NOT_MET   = "not_met"
    WAIVED    = "waived"   # approved exception for this criterion
    DEFERRED  = "deferred" # data not yet available; graduation blocked until data arrives

@dataclass
class GraduationCriterion:
    criterion_id: str
    name: str
    metric: str              # what is measured
    threshold: str           # the value or condition that constitutes "met"
    rationale: str           # why this criterion matters for graduation
    blocker: bool = True     # if True, failing this criterion blocks graduation

    # Filled in at review time
    actual_value: str = ""
    status: CriterionStatus = CriterionStatus.DEFERRED
    evaluator: str = ""      # who assessed this criterion
    notes: str = ""

    def blocks_graduation(self) -> bool:
        return self.blocker and self.status == CriterionStatus.NOT_MET

    def summary_line(self) -> str:
        status_str = self.status.value.upper()
        blocker_tag = " [BLOCKER]" if self.blocks_graduation() else ""
        return f"{self.criterion_id}: {self.name} — {status_str}{blocker_tag} ({self.actual_value} vs {self.threshold})"

The blocker field separates must-have graduation criteria from nice-to-have quality signals. A criterion marked as a blocker means that a NOT_MET status prevents graduation regardless of how well other criteria perform. A non-blocker criterion is still evaluated and recorded, but a miss does not prevent graduation — it informs the post-graduation improvement backlog. Teams should have no more than five blocker criteria; more than five blockers shifts the graduation gate from a quality check into a feature freeze.
Standard Graduation Criteria for Claude Integrations

Claude integrations share a common set of graduation concerns that differ from standard software features. The model interaction layer introduces quality dimensions that are not present in deterministic code: response quality varies with prompt design, model version, and user input distribution; safety behaviour must be validated against the real user population rather than just a synthetic test set; and cost per interaction in production typically differs from the pre-launch estimate because real user prompts are longer and more varied than test prompts. The standard criteria set covers all three concerns.

The unexpected refusal rate threshold deserves careful definition before the beta begins. An "unexpected" refusal is one that occurs on content that the operator's usage policy and system prompt explicitly permit — the model refused despite being configured to allow it. Expected refusals (on content that the system prompt is designed to refuse) should not be counted against this threshold. Tracking the two categories separately requires a refusal classification step in the feedback pipeline; without it, the operator cannot distinguish between a system prompt problem (unexpected refusal) and correct model behaviour (expected refusal).

The graduation decision is a structured record that documents the criteria evaluation, the forum's conclusion, and the rationale for any waivers. It serves as an audit trail for the product team, a communication artefact for stakeholders, and a reference for the post-graduation review team that will evaluate whether the graduation decision was correct at T+90.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from enum import Enum

class GraduationOutcome(str, Enum):
    APPROVED          = "approved"
    EXTENDED_BETA     = "extended_beta"
    RETIRED           = "retired"

@dataclass
class GraduationDecision:
    feature_name: str
    beta_start_date: date
    decision_date: date
    criteria: list[GraduationCriterion] = field(default_factory=list)
    outcome: GraduationOutcome = GraduationOutcome.EXTENDED_BETA
    decision_owner: str = ""       # DRI for the graduation decision
    forum_attendees: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    rationale: str = ""
    blockers_if_extended: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    next_review_date: date = None

    def blocking_failures(self) -> list[GraduationCriterion]:
        return [c for c in self.criteria if c.blocks_graduation()]

    def can_graduate(self) -> bool:
        return len(self.blocking_failures()) == 0

    def decision_summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"Feature: {self.feature_name}"]
        lines.append(f"Beta period: {self.beta_start_date} → {self.decision_date}")
        lines.append(f"Outcome: {self.outcome.value.upper()}")
        lines.append("")
        lines.append("Criteria summary:")
        for c in self.criteria:
            lines.append(f"  {c.summary_line()}")
        if self.blockers_if_extended:
            lines.append("")
            lines.append("Blockers to address before re-evaluation:")
            for b in self.blockers_if_extended:
                lines.append(f"  - {b}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

The decision_owner field records the DRI (Directly Responsible Individual) for the graduation decision. This is not the same as the person who ran the forum — it is the person who owns the outcome. If the feature is graduated and later causes a quality incident, the decision owner can review the graduation record to understand what evidence supported the graduation. If the feature is extended and the blocker list does not get addressed, the decision owner is accountable for following up. Without a named DRI, graduation decisions are collective and diffuse accountability, which means no one is tracking whether the blockers are being resolved.

The graduation forum is a structured meeting with a fixed agenda. It is not a product demo, a bug review, or a general retrospective. Its sole purpose is to evaluate each criterion against the data collected during the beta period and record an outcome. The meeting should be completable in 60 minutes for a standard integration; longer meetings are usually a symptom of criteria that were not defined precisely enough before the beta began, or of a team relitigating criteria at the graduation meeting that should have been settled in advance.

The data analyst prepares a one-page criteria scorecard with the actual value for each criterion. Attendees read it before the meeting so discussion time is spent on evaluation, not data presentation.
Walk through each blocker criterion. For each that is NOT_MET, the product manager explains whether it can be addressed within an extended beta cycle or whether it represents a fundamental issue.

Any waiver requests are reviewed. A waiver is a formal exception to a blocker criterion with documented justification. Waivers require explicit approval from the decision owner and are recorded in the graduation record.

Based on the blocker review and any approved waivers, the forum agrees on the outcome. If approved, the GA date and rollout plan are confirmed. If extended, the blocker list and next review date are recorded.
The decision owner captures the GraduationDecision record. The forum facilitator sends the outcome to stakeholders within 24 hours.

The waiver process is important to include explicitly. In practice, some criteria will be very close to the threshold — a positive feedback rate of 74.2% against a 75% threshold, for example. Without a waiver process, teams either apply arbitrary leniency (treating 74.2% as "close enough" without documentation) or strict rigidity (blocking graduation for a 0.8% miss that is within measurement noise). A formal waiver with documented justification preserves both flexibility and accountability: the team can graduate despite the near-miss, but the decision and its rationale are recorded and reviewable.

After the forum, the graduation decision must be communicated to three audiences: the engineering team, the internal stakeholders who were tracking the beta, and the beta users themselves. Each audience needs different information at different levels of detail.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class GraduationCommunicationPlan:
    feature_name: str
    graduation_date: str
    ga_rollout_percentage: int     # % of users who will get access on graduation day

    engineering_actions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    # e.g. ["remove beta flag", "update monitoring thresholds", "archive beta metrics"]

    stakeholder_message: str = ""
    # Summarises: outcome, GA date, rollout plan, key metrics from beta

    beta_user_message: str = ""
    # Tells users: feature is graduating, what changes (if anything), timeline

    def is_full_rollout(self) -> bool:
        return self.ga_rollout_percentage >= 100

    def communication_checklist(self) -> list[str]:
        items = [
            "Engineering actions list non-empty",
            "Stakeholder message drafted",
            "Beta user notification drafted (if beta users were explicitly recruited)",
        ]
        if not self.is_full_rollout():
            items.append(f"Phased rollout plan documented: {self.ga_rollout_percentage}% on day 1")
        return items

The ga_rollout_percentage field handles a common graduation pattern: a feature that was in limited beta with explicitly recruited users graduates to a phased general availability rather than a full rollout. A 25% rollout on graduation day followed by a 100% rollout two weeks later allows the team to verify that the integration performs at full scale before committing the full user population. Phased rollouts require the feature flag infrastructure to remain in place after graduation; features that graduate to 100% can remove the flag immediately.
Extended Beta Planning and Blocker Resolution

When the graduation forum votes to extend the beta, the outcome is only useful if the extension has a concrete plan attached to it. An extension without a plan is a deferral — the team has decided not to decide, and the feature will be re-evaluated at the next scheduled review with the same data and the same outcome. A genuine extension identifies the specific blockers, assigns owners for each, sets a target resolution date, and schedules a follow-up graduation review. The follow-up review is not a full forum — it is a targeted check that the blockers have been addressed.

The max_extension_cycles field prevents indefinite extension. If a feature has gone through two full extension cycles and still cannot meet the blocker criteria, the graduation forum should revisit whether the criteria are correct or whether the feature has a fundamental issue that additional beta time cannot resolve. Two failed extension cycles with the same blocker is strong evidence that the blocker is not being addressed effectively — either the root cause diagnosis is wrong, the proposed fix is insufficient, or the feature should be retired.

Graduation changes the operational context of a Claude integration. In beta, the team actively monitors the integration and is prepared to intervene quickly; early adopters understand they are using a non-final product and have higher tolerance for issues. In general availability, the integration must run under normal SLOs, be supported by the standard on-call rotation, and be backed by the customer-facing support process. The graduation event is the trigger for a set of operational handoff tasks that must complete before or on the graduation date.
Restarts review cadence from GA date
Needed as comparison baseline at T+30

The most important handoff item for Claude integrations is adding the integration to the on-call runbook with Claude-specific failure modes documented. Generic runbooks tell the on-call engineer what to do when a service goes down; a Claude-specific section tells them what to do when the model starts refusing unexpectedly, when response quality degrades after a model version change, or when token costs spike due to a change in user behaviour. The on-call engineer handling a 2am incident cannot consult the feature team; the runbook is the only reference available.
Documenting the Graduation Record for Audit

The graduation decision record is an operational document that lives beyond the graduation event itself. It is referenced at the T+90 review to evaluate whether the graduation decision was correct given the data available at the time; it is referenced if a post-graduation incident occurs on a criterion that was close to the threshold; and it is referenced if a waiver was approved and the waived criterion later becomes a problem. Storing the record in a queryable format rather than a meeting notes document makes these lookups practical.

import json
from dataclasses import asdict, dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from pathlib import Path

def graduation_record_path(feature_name: str, base_dir: str = "graduation-records") -> Path:
    """
    Canonical storage path for a graduation record.
    One file per graduation event; updated if a re-graduation occurs.
    """
    slug = feature_name.lower().replace(" ", "-")
    return Path(base_dir) / f"{slug}-graduation.json"

def load_graduation_record(feature_name: str, base_dir: str = "graduation-records"):
    path = graduation_record_path(feature_name, base_dir)
    if not path.exists():
        return None
    with open(path) as f:
        return json.load(f)

def save_graduation_record(decision, base_dir: str = "graduation-records") -> Path:
    path = graduation_record_path(decision.feature_name, base_dir)
    path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(asdict(decision), f, indent=2, default=str)
    return path

def audit_trail_summary(feature_name: str, base_dir: str = "graduation-records") -> str:
    record = load_graduation_record(feature_name, base_dir)
    if not record is None:
        outcome = record.get("outcome", "unknown")
        date_val = record.get("decision_date", "unknown")
        owner = record.get("decision_owner", "unassigned")
        return f"{feature_name}: {outcome.upper()} on {date_val} (DRI: {owner})"
    return f"{feature_name}: no graduation record found"

The canonical path convention means any tool — the T+90 review dashboard, the incident response runbook, the decommission checklist — can load a feature's graduation record without needing to know where it was stored. Using a slug derived from the feature name rather than a UUID makes the records human-browsable: an engineer investigating an incident can find the graduation record for a feature named "Code Suggestions" in the file code-suggestions-graduation.json without needing to query a registry. The graduation record is immutable after the decision is signed off; a re-graduation after an extended beta creates a new record with a new decision date rather than overwriting the original.

Beta graduation is a gate, not a timeline. The GraduationCriterion and GraduationDecision structures encode the criteria and the decision into a machine-readable record that eliminates post-hoc threshold-setting and creates an audit trail for the T+90 review. The standard criteria set for Claude integrations covers positive feedback rate, unexpected refusal rate, API error rate, latency, cost, regeneration rate, and safety incidents — with the four stability and quality criteria as hard blockers and the two cost and usability criteria as non-blockers. The forum runs in 60 minutes with a fixed agenda: pre-read scorecard, blocker review, waiver discussion, outcome decision, and communication. The GraduationCommunicationPlan structures the three post-decision communications to engineering, stakeholders, and beta users.
